title: Inference Economics Watcher
subtitle: Public-source tracker of the cost × capability × latency frontier for inference — across frontier labs and third-party providers. Methodology declared up front. Half-year baseline, then quarterly revisions as benchmarks saturate.
scope: Frontier and near-frontier models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, xAI, DeepSeek, Qwen, Mistral. Third-party inference providers hosting the same model families: AWS Bedrock, Azure, Vertex AI, Together, Fireworks, Groq, Cerebras. Seeded from published pricing pages, benchmark leaderboards, and official lab/provider announcements through April 2026.
sourceCoverage: April 2026 baseline from public pricing and benchmark sources.
objective: Track the cost × capability × latency frontier of inference over time, so a reader at any period check-in can see which models sit on the pareto frontier, how the frontier is moving, and what the $/capability-point ratio looks like at a chosen benchmark.
successCondition: A reader opening the doc at the end of any monitoring period knows the methodology in force, the current position of each tracked model on cost × capability, the direction of travel on cost, latency, and capability indicators, and the major pricing or launch moves of the period.

objectiveFacets.name: Methodology declared
objectiveFacets.description: Benchmark choices, cost axes, and latency axes are declared in a decision-record at the top of the doc; changes to the methodology are themselves new decision-record entries.

objectiveFacets.name: Frontier map current
objectiveFacets.description: Every tracked model has a position on the cost × capability map and its prior-period position preserved.

objectiveFacets.name: Indicators current
objectiveFacets.description: Cost, latency, and capability indicators carry a latestValue with an as-of date and a source.

objectiveFacets.name: Price and launch moves logged
objectiveFacets.description: Every major pricing change, launch, or access-tier shift in the period is recorded with dateOf, byActor, intent, and observed effect.

objectiveFacets.name: Period attribution
objectiveFacets.description: Every update is attributable to a named monitoring period.

periods.summary: Consolidated baseline as of mid-April 2026. Eight frontier and near-frontier models placed on the cost × GPQA map; four inference providers tracked; five pricing/launch moves logged; seven indicators dashboarded. Quarterly cadence after this — next revision 2026-Q3.

invariants.name: Methodology cites a choice
invariants.statement: The methodology decision-record must declare which benchmarks, which cost axes, and which latency axes are in force, with rationale for each.

invariants.name: Every model position is dated
invariants.statement: Every frontier-map card must carry an asOf date matching the period window.

invariants.name: Every move has a dateOf and byActor
invariants.statement: Every strategic-move-log card must carry a dateOf and a byActor reference.

invariants.name: Every indicator is dated
invariants.statement: Every indicator card must have a latestValue with an as-of date and a source.

invariants.name: Every source has a publication date
invariants.statement: Every citation card must carry a publishedAt field.

callouts.title: What this tracker is and isn't
items: Built entirely from public sources — published pricing pages, official benchmark claims, provider leaderboards (artificialanalysis.ai, vellum.ai leaderboard, Scale SEAL), mainstream press coverage. No NDA figures.

Prices are baseline directional values — real cost depends on batch discounts, cached input, negotiated enterprise rates, and self-hosted economics. Where a number is public and precise, it cites the source; where directional, the prose says so.
Benchmarks saturate. The methodology-decision-record declares the current benchmark set and what the policy is when a benchmark saturates (rotate, retire, or re-weight).

Quarterly cadence: the pareto frontier moves meaningfully on quarter timescales, not weekly. Weekly cadence would fire on noise. Run /competitor-sync docs/inference-economics-watcher.json 2026-Q3 at the start of Q3.

sections.title: Methodology in force (2026-H1)
sections.rationale: Everything downstream in this doc depends on these choices. A change to any of them is a new decision-record entry, not an edit — the prior methodology stays readable so a later reader knows what a given period's numbers actually compared.

data.name: Benchmark set: GPQA Diamond + SWE-bench Verified + AIME 2025

notes.text: Three benchmarks tracked, each chosen for still being discriminating at the top in 2026-H1. GPQA Diamond (graduate-level science Q&A) separates frontier from near-frontier. SWE-bench Verified (real GitHub bug fixes) reads coding-agent capability, the workload most builders pay for. AIME 2025 (math olympiad) reads reasoning separately from knowledge.

notes.title: Saturation policy
items: If a tracked benchmark exceeds 95% top-score, it rotates out in the next period and a replacement is declared in a new decision-record entry.
GPQA Diamond currently sits around 85–88% at frontier — discriminating but approaching rotation. Candidate replacements: Humanity's Last Exam, FrontierMath.
SWE-bench Verified sits around 70–75% at frontier — comfortable mid-discrimination zone.
AIME 2025 sits above 90% at frontier — rotation candidate next period; replacement TBD from the 2026 AMO or USAMO pools.
Benchmark rotation is a methodology change, not a data update — when it happens, a new decision-record card appears and the capability-indicators series annotates the rotation.

data.name: Cost axes: list $/M input, list $/M output, cached $/M input

notes.text: List $/M input and list $/M output are the published per-million-token rates for each model at its default tier on its primary API. Cached $/M input is the discounted rate for cache hits where the provider exposes one — Anthropic and OpenAI explicitly; Google implicit via context caching API. Batch-mode 50% discounts are noted in prose but not tracked as their own axis.

notes.title: What we deliberately don't track as axes
items: Self-hosted economics on rented GPUs (highly workload-dependent; tracked in prose where publicly claimed).
Third-party provider rates for the same model family (tracked as a separate provider axis in the providers section, not rolled into list price).
Per-task cost rollups (depend on prompt engineering and agentic loops; out of scope for this doc).

data.name: Latency axes: TTFT at 1k-context, sustained throughput (output tokens/sec)

notes.text: TTFT (time to first token) is measured at 1k-token context on a warm connection — the closest proxy to interactive-agent experience. Sustained throughput is output tokens/sec at steady state. p99 and cold-start figures are mentioned in prose where relevant but not tracked as primary axes — they vary too much by region and load.

Where the same model is served by multiple providers with materially different throughput (Groq, Cerebras, SambaNova vs. first-party), the provider-specific numbers are captured on the provider's own card. First-party numbers drive the model-level card.

sections.title: Tracked models
sections.rationale: Eight frontier and near-frontier models tracked by 2026-H1 profile — model family, origin lab, pricing tier, primary capability claim, and the workload it's positioned for. Models are the actors here, not labs, because pricing and capability are model-scoped.

data.name: GPT-5 (OpenAI)
data.stage: GA on OpenAI API · Azure OpenAI · late 2025 launch
data.strategicPosture: Frontier capability at mid-tier closed-weight pricing
data.currentBet: List pricing well below the Opus-4.7 tier while claiming competitive frontier benchmarks. Position the flagship as the default choice for builders who want top capability without paying the Opus premium.
activeInitiatives: GPT-5 family rollout across ChatGPT tiers and API
Aggressive cache-discount tier for agentic workloads
Operator agent and memory product bundling

notes.text: OpenAI pricing page and GPT-5 launch blog, Q1 2026.

data.name: GPT-5 mini (OpenAI)
data.currentBet: An order of magnitude cheaper than GPT-5, within striking distance on most benchmarks, targeting builders whose workload doesn't need frontier capability.
activeInitiatives: Mini-tier positioning against Gemini 3 Flash and Haiku 4.5

notes.text: OpenAI pricing page, Q1 2026.

data.name: Claude Opus 4.7 (Anthropic)
data.stage: GA on Anthropic API · AWS Bedrock · Google Vertex
data.currentBet: Hold the premium price point (roughly 10× the GPT-5 tier on output) while leading on coding (SWE-bench), long-context reliability, and MCP-native tool use. Position Opus as the model you pick when correctness matters more than cost.
activeInitiatives: 1M-context GA across API and Claude Code
Aggressive prompt-cache discount to reduce effective cost for agent loops
MCP-native tool use as a capability advantage at frontier

notes.text: Anthropic pricing page and Opus 4.7 launch blog, Q1 2026.

data.name: Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Anthropic)
data.stage: GA on Anthropic API · AWS Bedrock · Google Vertex
data.currentBet: Hold the mid-tier price point against GPT-5 and Gemini 3 Pro while matching them on coding and reasoning benchmarks — the workhorse for agentic workloads that can't justify Opus economics.
activeInitiatives: Claude Code CLI default model
MCP-native tool use preserved at mid-tier pricing

notes.text: Anthropic pricing page, Q1 2026.

data.name: Gemini 3 Pro (Google)
data.sector: Frontier · closed-weight · integrated with Google distribution
data.stage: GA on Vertex AI · AI Studio
data.strategicPosture: Frontier capability wired into Google's product stack
data.currentBet: Price Gemini 3 Pro competitively against GPT-5 on list; lean on native integration with Search, Workspace, and Android for builder pull-through. TPU capacity as a margin lever not yet fully visible in pricing.
activeInitiatives: Gemini 3 family GA on Vertex AI
Context caching API adoption for long-context workloads

notes.text: Google Cloud Vertex AI pricing page, Q1 2026.

data.name: Gemini 3 Flash (Google)
data.stage: GA on Vertex AI · AI Studio
data.currentBet: Price Flash well below GPT-5 mini and Haiku 4.5 to win high-volume / latency-sensitive workloads. TPU economics as the structural advantage behind the aggressive pricing.
activeInitiatives: Flash-as-default for multimodal volume workloads
Speculative-decoding deployment for throughput headline

notes.text: Google Cloud Vertex AI pricing page, Q1 2026.

data.name: Llama 4 Maverick (Meta · open-weight)
data.sector: Near-frontier · open-weight · hosted by third-party providers
data.stage: Open-weight release; hosted on Together, Fireworks, Groq, AWS Bedrock
data.strategicPosture: Ecosystem lever for Meta, commodity inference for everyone else
data.currentBet: Meta ships the weights; third-party providers compete on speed and price. For builders this means a near-frontier option at open-weight economics with provider-choice leverage — no single vendor lock-in.
activeInitiatives: Llama 4 family open-weights release
Provider-side speed competition (Groq, Cerebras, SambaNova)

notes.text: Meta Llama release notes and Together/Fireworks/Groq pricing pages, Q1 2026.

data.name: DeepSeek V3.2 (DeepSeek · open-weight)
data.stage: Open-weight release; hosted by DeepSeek + Together + Fireworks
data.geography: Global via third-party providers; China-origin lab
data.currentBet: Ship frontier-competitive capability at roughly an order of magnitude lower cost than Western closed frontier. Forces the cost conversation across the whole market — an exogenous shock to frontier-lab unit economics.
activeInitiatives: V3.2 release at aggressively low $/M output rates
MIT-style open-weight license broadening downstream adoption

notes.text: DeepSeek blog posts and hosted-provider pricing pages, Q1 2026.

sections.title: Tracked inference providers
sections.rationale: Inference providers as a distinct actor class — they don't train frontier models but they sell them, sometimes faster or cheaper than the model's origin. Tracked separately because their competitive dynamic (speed, multi-model catalogue, price-per-token race) is different from a lab's.

data.name: Together AI
data.strategicPosture: Multi-model open-weight catalogue at aggressive prices
data.currentBet: Host every meaningful open-weight model (Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek, Mistral) at competitive prices; win on breadth + price. Serverless + dedicated-endpoint hybrid.
activeInitiatives: Day-zero hosting of major open-weight releases
Dedicated-endpoint motion for enterprise workloads

notes.text: Together.ai pricing page and blog, Q1 2026.

data.name: Fireworks AI
data.strategicPosture: Speed-focused open-weight host plus fine-tuning platform
data.currentBet: Differentiate on serving efficiency (speculative decoding, batching) and on fine-tuning / custom-model hosting rather than pure catalogue breadth.
activeInitiatives: FireOptimizer for low-latency serving
Fine-tuning-and-host motion for enterprise

notes.text: Fireworks.ai pricing page, Q1 2026.

data.name: Groq
data.currentBet: Custom LPU inference silicon delivering tokens/sec figures an order of magnitude above GPU-based providers; position as the default for latency-sensitive agentic workloads.
activeInitiatives: Llama 4 Maverick served at multi-thousand-tokens/sec headline
LPU data-center buildout for capacity

notes.text: Groq pricing and throughput announcements, Q1 2026.

data.name: AWS Bedrock
data.strategicPosture: Managed multi-model platform tied to AWS workloads
data.currentBet: Host Anthropic, Meta, Mistral, Cohere, Amazon's own Nova family inside AWS identity, networking, and compliance — win on enterprise gravity rather than price.
activeInitiatives: Bedrock pricing passthrough for Anthropic and Llama 4
Provisioned-throughput tier for enterprise workloads

notes.text: AWS Bedrock pricing page, Q1 2026.

sections.title: Pricing and launch moves this period
sections.rationale: Discrete actions by labs and providers that reshaped the cost × capability frontier in 2026-H1 — new model launches, price cuts, speculative-decoding rollouts, cache-discount changes.

data.name: OpenAI launches GPT-5 family with sub-GPT-4o list pricing
data.intent: Establish the frontier-capability-at-mid-tier-cost position and pressure Anthropic's Opus-tier margins.
data.observedEffect: Broad market pressure on mid-tier pricing; Gemini 3 Pro responded with matching list rates.

notes.text: OpenAI pricing page and launch blog, January 2026.

data.name: DeepSeek V3.2 ships at order-of-magnitude lower cost than frontier closed
data.intent: Demonstrate near-frontier capability at Chinese-stack cost economics; forces a public cost conversation across the whole market.
data.observedEffect: Visible downward pressure on open-weight hosted prices (Together, Fireworks), and public discussion of closed-frontier margin structure.

notes.text: DeepSeek blog and press coverage, February 2026.

data.name: Anthropic raises prompt-cache discount to 90% on Opus / Sonnet
data.intent: Make the Opus-tier economics viable for long-running agent loops (where the same system prompt is served repeatedly) without cutting list price.
data.observedEffect: Effective cost for cached agentic workloads drops sharply; MCP-heavy deployments see real-world cost decline even at unchanged headline rate.

notes.text: Anthropic pricing page update, March 2026.

data.name: Groq serves Llama 4 Maverick at multi-thousand-tokens/sec headline
data.intent: Establish an order-of-magnitude throughput lead on open-weight inference; own the latency-sensitive segment.
data.observedEffect: Agentic workloads with real-time constraints visibly shift toward Groq for Llama-4-class models; first-party and GPU-based providers respond with speculative-decoding rollouts.

notes.text: Groq blog and benchmark coverage, March 2026.

data.name: Google prices Gemini 3 Flash aggressively for multimodal volume
data.intent: Win high-volume multimodal workloads with TPU-economics pricing that closed competitors can't easily match on GPU-based infrastructure.
data.observedEffect: GPT-5 mini and Haiku 4.5 see volume-workload pressure; open-weight + Groq combinations the main defensive response.

notes.text: Google Cloud Vertex AI pricing page and launch blog, February 2026.

sections.title: Cost indicators
sections.rationale: Price-side indicators tracked across periods. Values carry as text with a source; trend is categorical.

data.name: Median $/M output tokens at frontier (top 5 models by GPQA)
data.latestValue: ~$8/M output (median of Opus 4.7, GPT-5, Gemini 3 Pro, Sonnet 4.6, and the best open-weight frontier on Together/Fireworks)
data.forecast: Continued decline expected if DeepSeek-style open-weight pressure persists and Gemini 3 Flash holds its aggressive Flash-tier pricing.

notes.text: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Together, Fireworks pricing pages, Q1–Q2 2026.

data.name: Open-weight vs closed-weight cost gap (same-capability tier)
data.latestValue: Open-weight near-frontier (Llama 4 Maverick, DeepSeek V3.2 on Together/Fireworks) is roughly 5–15× cheaper per M output than the closest-capability closed frontier
data.forecast: Gap likely persists or widens as open-weight hosts compete on price; closed frontier defends on capability and ecosystem, not cost.

notes.text: Together and Fireworks pricing vs first-party frontier pricing pages, Q1–Q2 2026.

data.name: Cache-discount prevalence across frontier labs
data.latestValue: Three of four frontier labs (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) now offer a cache / context-caching discount of 75–90% on repeated input tokens
data.forecast: Near-universal adoption expected by end of 2026; agentic workloads' effective cost will diverge sharply from headline list price.

notes.text: Anthropic, OpenAI, Google pricing page updates, Q1 2026.

sections.title: Latency indicators
sections.rationale: Speed-side indicators — TTFT at 1k-context and sustained throughput at warm state.

data.name: Frontier TTFT at 1k-context (median across frontier closed-weight models, first-party API)
data.forecast: Continued decline likely as speculative-decoding and context-caching adoption broaden.

notes.text: artificialanalysis.ai latency tracking, Q1–Q2 2026.

data.name: Frontier output throughput (tokens/sec, best provider per model family)
data.latestValue: First-party GPU-based: ~80–150 tok/s at frontier. Groq LPU on open-weight: ~2000+ tok/s headline. Cerebras/SambaNova also in the high-hundreds on open-weight.
data.forecast: Custom-silicon lead on open-weight persists; closed-frontier throughput rises more slowly but steadily.

notes.text: artificialanalysis.ai throughput tracking and provider benchmark pages, Q1–Q2 2026.

sections.title: Capability indicators
sections.rationale: Top-score tracking per benchmark in the current methodology. One indicator per benchmark, plus the open-vs-closed frontier gap.

data.name: Top GPQA Diamond score (any model)
data.asOf: 2026-04-20 · lab blogs + Scale SEAL / Vellum leaderboards
data.forecast: Saturation approaching; benchmark rotation likely for 2026-Q3.

notes.text: GPQA leaderboards and lab-published scores, Q1–Q2 2026.

data.name: Top SWE-bench Verified score (any model)
data.forecast: Rising toward 80%+ plausible within the year if agentic scaffolding improves at current rate; still discriminating into 2026-Q3.

notes.text: SWE-bench Verified leaderboard and lab blog claims, Q1–Q2 2026.

data.name: Top AIME 2025 score (any model)
data.forecast: Saturation imminent; rotation candidate for 2026-Q3. Candidate replacement: USAMO or FrontierMath.

notes.text: Lab-published AIME 2025 results, Q1–Q2 2026.

data.name: Open-weight vs closed-weight capability gap (GPQA Diamond)
data.latestValue: ~8–12 points behind closed frontier (Llama 4 Maverick and DeepSeek V3.2 vs Opus 4.7 / GPT-5 / Gemini 3 Pro)
data.forecast: Gap has been closing slowly; Meta Superintelligence Labs and DeepSeek next-gen could accelerate or flatten it.

notes.text: Public GPQA leaderboards and Hugging Face, Q1–Q2 2026.

sections.title: Frontier map — cost × GPQA Diamond
sections.rationale: X-axis: list $/M output tokens (log scale implied — order-of-magnitude steps). Y-axis: GPQA Diamond score (points). The pareto frontier is the curve connecting the lowest-cost model at each capability tier; it is implicit in the positions and is noted in each card where a model sits on it.

data.name: Gemini 3 Flash
data.axisY: ~70% GPQA Diamond · near-frontier · on the cost-side pareto frontier

data.name: Llama 4 Maverick (Together/Fireworks)
data.axisY: ~73% GPQA Diamond · near-frontier · on the cost-side pareto frontier

data.name: DeepSeek V3.2 (DeepSeek/Together/Fireworks)
data.axisY: ~75% GPQA Diamond · near-frontier · cost-side pareto frontier leader

sections.title: Period notes
sections.rationale: One card per monitoring period summarising what shifted on the frontier, what resolved, and what's worth watching into the next period.

notes.text: Consolidated baseline as of mid-April 2026. Methodology declared (GPQA Diamond + SWE-bench Verified + AIME 2025; list $/M in/out + cached input; TTFT at 1k-context + sustained throughput). Eight frontier and near-frontier models placed on the cost × GPQA map. Four providers tracked. Five moves logged: GPT-5 mid-tier pricing launch, DeepSeek V3.2 open-weight cost shock, Anthropic cache-discount raise, Groq multi-thousand-tok/s on Llama 4, Gemini 3 Flash aggressive volume pricing. Seven indicators across cost, latency, and capability. First full pareto frontier captured with open-weight tier (DeepSeek, Llama 4, Gemini 3 Flash) leading the cost-side frontier at near-frontier capability.

notes.title: Focus for 2026-Q3
items: Does AIME 2025 need to rotate out (saturation)? Declare replacement if so.
Does the open-vs-closed capability gap close meaningfully when the next Llama / DeepSeek / Qwen ship?
Do speculative-decoding rollouts at OpenAI / Anthropic / Google close the Groq-LPU throughput gap?
Does cache-discount adoption go universal (force multiplier on effective cost declines)?
Does any provider ship a cost × capability position that isn't already on the map as a known cluster?
